title: 2009 NFL season

infobox.value: [Sun Life Stadium](/wiki/Sun_Life_Stadium "Sun Life Stadium"), [Miami Gardens, Florida](/wiki/Miami_Gardens,_Florida "Miami Gardens, Florida")

was the 90th season in the history of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL). The 50th anniversary of the original eight charter members of the [American Football League](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League") was celebrated during this season.

**NFC teams: [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Yellow_ffff00_pog.svg/20px-Yellow_ffff00_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Yellow_ffff00_pog.svg) West, [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Blue_pog.svg/20px-Blue_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Blue_pog.svg) North, [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Red_pog.svg/20px-Red_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Red_pog.svg) South, [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/White_pog.svg/20px-White_pog.svg.png)](/wiki/File:White_pog.svg) East**

The **2009 NFL season** was the 90th season in the history of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL). The 50th anniversary of the original eight charter members of the [American Football League](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League") was celebrated during this season.

The [preseason](/wiki/Preseason "Preseason") started with the [Pro Football Hall of Fame Game](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame_Game "Pro Football Hall of Fame Game") on August 9, 2009,[[1]](#cite_note-1) and the [regular season](/wiki/Regular_season_(NFL) "Regular season (NFL)") began September 10, with the reigning [Super Bowl XLIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIII "Super Bowl XLIII") champion [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2009_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2009 Pittsburgh Steelers season") defeating the [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/2009_Tennessee_Titans_season "2009 Tennessee Titans season") in overtime. The season ended with [Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV"), the league's [championship game](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), on February 7, 2010, at [Sun Life Stadium](/wiki/Sun_Life_Stadium "Sun Life Stadium") with [the New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2009_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2009 New Orleans Saints season") defeating [the Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2009_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2009 Indianapolis Colts season")[[2]](#cite_note-2) 31–17 in [Miami Gardens, Florida](/wiki/Miami_Gardens,_Florida "Miami Gardens, Florida").[[3]](#cite_note-Super_Bowl_date-3) The Colts and Saints began the season 14–0 and 13–0 respectively. This was the first time in NFL history two teams started 13–0 or better.

## Draft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Draft")]

The [2009 NFL draft](/wiki/2009_NFL_draft "2009 NFL draft") was held from April 25 to 26, 2009, at [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")'s [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall"). ## Referee change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Referee change")]

[Bill Carollo](/wiki/Bill_Carollo "Bill Carollo") resigned to become the Director of Officiating for the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference"). [Don Carey](/wiki/Don_Carey_(American_football_official) "Don Carey (American football official)"), brother of NFL referee [Mike Carey](/wiki/Mike_Carey_(American_football) "Mike Carey (American football)"), was promoted from back judge to take Carollo's place.

## Rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rule changes")]

Several rule changes were passed at the league's annual owners meeting in [Dana Point, California](/wiki/Dana_Point,_California "Dana Point, California"), during the week of March 23.

The following rules were passed to improve player safety and reduce injuries:[[23]](#cite_note-Rules0324-23)

* A blindside block cannot be initially delivered by a helmet, forearm or shoulder to an opponent's head or neck.
* The initial contact to the head of a defenseless receiver is also prohibited.
* On kickoffs, a blocking wedge cannot consist of more than two players.
* During onside kickoff attempts, the kicking team cannot have more than five players bunched together.
* Clarified the [2006](/wiki/2006_NFL_season "2006 NFL season") rule about hitting passers below the knees; a defender on the ground cannot lunge or dive at or below the passer's knees. This is unofficially referred to as the "[Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") Rule", after Brady was injured at the [Patriots' 2008](/wiki/2008_New_England_Patriots_season "2008 New England Patriots season") opening game against the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2008_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2008 Kansas City Chiefs season"), when Chiefs safety [Bernard Pollard](/wiki/Bernard_Pollard "Bernard Pollard") hit Brady below the knees, sidelining him for the rest of the 2008 season.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The replay system will now be allowed to cover the following situations:[[25]](#cite_note-Rules0325-25)

* Whether a loose ball from a passer is a fumble or an incomplete pass.
* Whether a loose ball actually hit the sideline.

Other new rules included:[[25]](#cite_note-Rules0325-25)

* If onside kick does not go 10 yards, goes out of bounds, or is touched illegally at any time during the kick, the ball is immediately awarded to the receiving team. This amends a rule that was first implemented during the [2003 season](/wiki/2003_NFL_season#Major_rule_changes "2003 NFL season"). Previously, if the kicking team committed this foul before the final five minutes of the game, they had another chance to kick again from five yards back.
* On all fumbles and laterals that go out of bounds, the clock will immediately start when the referee signals ready for play instead of waiting until next snap.
* After a punt hit the center-hung video display boards during a pre-season game, the league temporarily modified the rule regarding balls in play that strike an object such as a video board or a guy wire: in addition for the down being replayed, the game clock will also be reset to the time when the play was snapped.[[26]](#cite_note-ruleDALStadium-26)
* In November the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") held hearings regarding NFL players on the field receiving concussions and other major injuries. Strong recommendations were made to the commissioner, and on December 2, 2009, NFL Commissioner [Roger Goodell](/wiki/Roger_Goodell "Roger Goodell") issued a memo effective immediately stating, in part: "Once removed for the duration of a practice or game, the player should not be considered for return-to-football activities until he is fully asymptotic, both at rest and after exertion, has a normal neurological examination, normal neuropsychological testing, and has been cleared to return by both his team physician(s) and the independent neurological consultant." The old standard, established in 2007, said a player shouldn't be allowed to return to the same game if he lost consciousness.[[27]](#cite_note-Goodellmemo-27)

## Preseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Preseason")]

The [Pro Football Hall of Fame Game](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame_Game "Pro Football Hall of Fame Game") was on August 9, 2009, at 8:00 pm EDT on [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"). The Tennessee Titans defeated the Buffalo Bills 21–18, and both wore "throwback" jerseys celebrating the two franchises' AFL origins.[[28]](#cite_note-hofg010609-28) It was the first time since 1970 that the teams have not been from opposing conferences; also, both Bills owner [Ralph Wilson](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson "Ralph Wilson"), a 2009 Hall of Fame inductee, and Titans owner [Bud Adams](/wiki/Bud_Adams "Bud Adams") have owned their teams continuously since the AFL's inception in 1960, making them the longest-tenured team owners in the league. Both teams made their first Hall of Fame Game appearance since the 1980s (Buffalo last played in Canton in [1989](/wiki/1989_NFL_season "1989 NFL season"), Tennessee in [1985](/wiki/1985_NFL_season "1985 NFL season") as the former [Houston Oilers](/wiki/Houston_Oilers "Houston Oilers")).

The rest of the [pre-season](/wiki/National_Football_League_exhibition_season "National Football League exhibition season") matchups were announced March 30, 2009. Highlights, among others, included a rematch of [Super Bowl XLIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIII "Super Bowl XLIII") between the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") and the [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals") on ESPN.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Regular season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Regular season")]

The 2009 season began on September 10, 2009. Under the current scheduling system, this is the latest date the NFL can start its season as the season typically starts the weekend after Labor Day, which falls on its latest possible date in 2009.

For the 2009 season, the intraconference and interconference matchups were:

**Intraconference**

* [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") vs. [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South")
* [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") vs. [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West")
* [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") vs. [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South")
* [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North") vs. [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West")

**Interconference**

* [AFC East](/wiki/AFC_East "AFC East") vs. [NFC South](/wiki/NFC_South "NFC South")
* [AFC North](/wiki/AFC_North "AFC North") vs. [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North")
* [AFC South](/wiki/AFC_South "AFC South") vs. [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West")
* [AFC West](/wiki/AFC_West "AFC West") vs. [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East")

The 2009 schedule was released on April 14, 2009.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Highlights of the 2009 season included:

* **[NFL Kickoff Game](/wiki/NFL_Kickoff_Game "NFL Kickoff Game")**: The 2009 season began with the Kickoff Game on September 10, with the [Super Bowl XLIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIII "Super Bowl XLIII") champion [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") defeating the [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/Tennessee_Titans "Tennessee Titans") 13–10 in overtime.
* The Week 1 doubleheader for *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")* honored the 50th anniversary of the original eight charter members of the [American Football League](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League"),[[31]](#cite_note-31) with the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2009_New_England_Patriots_season "2009 New England Patriots season") defeating the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2009_Buffalo_Bills_season "2009 Buffalo Bills season") 25–24, and the [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/2009_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2009 San Diego Chargers season") defeating the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/2009_Oakland_Raiders_season "2009 Oakland Raiders season") 24–20.
* **[NFL International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series")**: The NFL International Series game was played on October 26, 2009, at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), featuring the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2009_New_England_Patriots_season "2009 New England Patriots season") defeating the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/2009_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "2009 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") 35–7.
* **[Thanksgiving](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day"):** Three games were contested on November 26, 2009.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2009_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2009 Green Bay Packers season") defeated the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2009_Detroit_Lions_season "2009 Detroit Lions season") 34–12, the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2009_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2009 Dallas Cowboys season") defeated the [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/2009_Oakland_Raiders_season "2009 Oakland Raiders season") 24–7, while in the primetime *[Thursday Night Football](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football")* game, the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/2009_Denver_Broncos_season "2009 Denver Broncos season") defeated the [New York Giants](/wiki/2009_New_York_Giants_season "2009 New York Giants season") 26–6..
* **[Bills Toronto Series](/wiki/Bills_Toronto_Series "Bills Toronto Series"):** The [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2009_Buffalo_Bills_season "2009 Buffalo Bills season") played the [New York Jets](/wiki/2009_New_York_Jets_season "2009 New York Jets season") at [Rogers Centre](/wiki/Rogers_Centre "Rogers Centre") in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") on December 3 on *Thursday Night Football*, with the Jets defeating the [cross-state rivals](/wiki/Bills%E2%80%93Jets_rivalry "Bills–Jets rivalry") 19–13. Earlier reports on a potential Bills-[Toronto Argonauts](/wiki/Toronto_Argonauts "Toronto Argonauts") doubleheader and reports of the league favoring other teams were proven inaccurate.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* **[Christmas](/wiki/National_Football_League_Christmas_games "National Football League Christmas games"):** As Christmas Eve fell on a Thursday, that week's *Thursday Night Football* game was scheduled as a rare Friday night game on Christmas Day,[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) featuring the [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/2009_San_Diego_Chargers_season "2009 San Diego Chargers season") defeating the [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/2009_Tennessee_Titans_season "2009 Tennessee Titans season") 42–17.

### Scheduling changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Scheduling changes")]

* **[China Bowl](/wiki/China_Bowl_(NFL) "China Bowl (NFL)")**: The China Bowl, a proposed [pre-season game](/wiki/National_Football_League_exhibition_season "National Football League exhibition season") between the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2009_New_England_Patriots_season "2009 New England Patriots season") and [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2009_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2009 Seattle Seahawks season"), would have taken place in [Beijing, China](/wiki/Beijing,_China "Beijing, China"). It had been postponed from the [2007 season](/wiki/2007_NFL_season "2007 NFL season") to 2009, but after the [2008 economic crisis](/wiki/Late_2000s_recession "Late 2000s recession"), the Patriots closed their Chinese operations and the game was cancelled.[[38]](#cite_note-38)
* The NFL moved the September 27 game between the [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/2009_Tennessee_Titans_season "2009 Tennessee Titans season") and [New York Jets](/wiki/2009_New_York_Jets_season "2009 New York Jets season") at [Giants Stadium](/wiki/Giants_Stadium "Giants Stadium") from 4:15 pm EDT to 1:00 pm EDT, in response to a request from Jets owner [Woody Johnson](/wiki/Woody_Johnson "Woody Johnson") to change the time to accommodate the Jewish [Yom Kippur](/wiki/Yom_Kippur "Yom Kippur") holiday that began at sundown that evening. This marked a rare occasion when the Jets and Giants played at the same time (the Giants played at [Tampa Bay](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers")).[[39]](#cite_note-39) In general, both New York City teams are not scheduled to play at the same time. The Jets game aired on CBS ([WCBS-TV](/wiki/WCBS-TV "WCBS-TV") in New York), while the Giants game aired on Fox ([WNYW-TV](/wiki/WNYW-TV "WNYW-TV")).
* The Jets and Giants played again at the same time on November 1. The Jets hosted the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") at Giants Stadium, while the Giants played at [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles"). The Giants–Eagles game was originally scheduled for 4:15 pm EST, but moved forward to 1:00 pm EST in order to avoid ending too close to the start of the [Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies") hosting Game 4 of the [2009 World Series](/wiki/2009_World_Series "2009 World Series") that evening at [Citizens Bank Park](/wiki/Citizens_Bank_Park "Citizens Bank Park"), which is directly across Pattison Avenue from [Lincoln Financial Field](/wiki/Lincoln_Financial_Field "Lincoln Financial Field"). The [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings")–[Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") game, originally scheduled for 1:00 pm EST that same day, was moved to 4:15 pm EST. It marked Brett Favre's return to Green Bay, this time as the Vikings quarterback.[[40]](#cite_note-40)
* The [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears")–Minnesota and [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals")–Tennessee games on November 29 were rescheduled from 1:00 pm EST to 4:15 pm EST.
* By way of [Flex Scheduling](/wiki/Flex_Scheduling "Flex Scheduling"), the Minnesota–Arizona game on December 6 was moved to the 8:20 pm EST slot to replace the New England–Miami matchup.
* The [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams")–Tennessee game on December 13 was rescheduled from 1:00 pm EST to 4:05 pm EST.
* The Green Bay–[Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") game on December 20 was rescheduled from 1:00 pm EST to 4:15 pm EST. That same day, a major east coast blizzard forced the Chicago–[Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") and [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers")–Philadelphia games to be pushed back from 1:00 pm EST to 4:15 pm EST on only two days notice, to allow more time for cleaning up the stadiums after the blizzard.[[41]](#cite_note-41)
* The [Denver](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos")–Philadelphia game on December 27 was moved from 1:00 pm EST to 4:15 pm EST.
* For week 17, the Philadelphia–[Dallas](/wiki/2009_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2009 Dallas Cowboys season") game to decide the NFC East champion was switched to 4:15 pm EST, while the [Cincinnati](/wiki/2009_Cincinnati_Bengals_season "2009 Cincinnati Bengals season")–New York Jets game was selected as the final Sunday Night Football game.

### Week 17: End of team randomization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Week 17: End of team randomization")]

2009 marked the last time that Week 17 games could be randomized. Beginning in 2010, the Week 17 schedule was restricted to divisional match-ups only, in an attempt to increase competition after several cases over the last few seasons of playoff-bound teams resting their regular starters and playing their reserves. This has continued since then.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## Postseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Postseason")]

Main article: [2009–10 NFL playoffs](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_NFL_playoffs "2009–10 NFL playoffs")

The playoffs began Saturday, January 9, 2010, with Wild Card Weekend. Divisional playoffs followed the next week. The defending world champions [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2009_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2009 Pittsburgh Steelers season") did not take part in the post-season as they were eliminated from contention in the final week 17, thus this was the fifth year in a row the NFL crowned a new [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") champion.

The [AFC Championship Game](/wiki/AFC_Championship_Game "AFC Championship Game") was Sunday, January 24, 2010, at 3:00 pm, which saw the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") come from behind to defeat the [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets"), 30–17. It was followed by the [NFC Championship Game](/wiki/NFC_Championship_Game "NFC Championship Game") at 6:30 pm which featured many back to back scoring drives by two high scoring Vikings and Saints offenses. But the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings") 4 fumbles and a [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre") interception late in the fourth quarter proved to be too much to handle as the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") won 31–28 in overtime which granted the franchise's first Super Bowl appearance in its 43-year history. [Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV") was held February 7 at [Miami Gardens, Florida](/wiki/Miami_Gardens,_Florida "Miami Gardens, Florida")'s [Sun Life Stadium](/wiki/Sun_Life_Stadium "Sun Life Stadium") (home of host team [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/2009_Miami_Dolphins_season "2009 Miami Dolphins season"), who were also eliminated from post-season contention).

The [2010 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2010_Pro_Bowl "2010 Pro Bowl") was held on January 31, one week *before* [Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV"), at the same site of the league championship game, Sun Life Stadium in Miami Gardens, Florida.[[45]](#cite_note-NFL.comProBowlMove-58) This was the first time since [1979](/wiki/1979_Pro_Bowl "1979 Pro Bowl") (held for the [1978](/wiki/1978_NFL_season "1978 NFL season") season) that the Pro Bowl was held in the [Continental United States](/wiki/Continental_United_States "Continental United States") as opposed to Hawaii. The NFL also announced that the site and date of Pro Bowl games after 2010 will include playing the game on a rotating basis in [Honolulu](/wiki/Honolulu "Honolulu").[[45]](#cite_note-NFL.comProBowlMove-58) NFL Commissioner [Roger Goodell](/wiki/Roger_Goodell "Roger Goodell") said the move was made after looking at alternatives to strengthen the Pro Bowl and to make the end of the season more climactic.[[45]](#cite_note-NFL.comProBowlMove-58) As a result of the move, players will not be allowed to play in both the Super Bowl and the Pro Bowl in the same year. In addition, ESPN replaced CBS as broadcaster for that game only.[[46]](#cite_note-ESPN.comProBowl-59)

Within each conference, the four division winners and the top two [non-division winners with the best overall regular season records)](/wiki/Wild_card_(sports) "Wild card (sports)") qualified for the playoffs. The four division winners are [seeded](/wiki/Seed_(sports) "Seed (sports)") 1–4 based on their overall won-lost-tied record, and the wild card teams are seeded 5–6. The NFL does not use a fixed bracket playoff system, and there are no restrictions regarding teams from the same division matching up in any round. In the first round, dubbed the *wild-card playoffs* or *wild-card weekend*, the third-seeded division winner hosts the sixth-seed wild card, and the fourth seed hosts the fifth. The 1 and 2 seeds from each conference received a [first-round bye](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)"). In the second round, the *divisional playoffs*, the number 1 seed hosts the worst-surviving seed from the first round (seed 4, 5, or 6), while the number 2 seed will play the other team (seed 3, 4, or 5). The two surviving teams from each conference's divisional playoff games met in the respective AFC and NFC Conference Championship games, hosted by the higher seed. Although the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), the championship round of the playoffs, is played at a neutral site, the designated home team is based on an annual rotation by conference.[[47]](#cite_note-yahoo-60)

## Fiftieth anniversary of the American Football League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Fiftieth anniversary of the American Football League")]

The 2009 season marked the fiftieth season of nine of the league's 32 teams: the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys"), and the Original Eight charter members of the [American Football League](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League"), whose owners became collectively known as "The [Foolish Club](/wiki/Foolish_Club "Foolish Club")". The fifth league to use the [AFL moniker](/wiki/American_Football_League_(disambiguation) "American Football League (disambiguation)") (previous leagues in [1926](/wiki/American_Football_League_(1926) "American Football League (1926)"), [1934](/wiki/American_Football_League_(1934) "American Football League (1934)"), [1936–37](/wiki/American_Football_League_(1936) "American Football League (1936)"), and [1940–41](/wiki/American_Football_League_(1940) "American Football League (1940)"), all had failed) began play in [1960](/wiki/1960_American_Football_League_season "1960 American Football League season") and would form the major portion of the [American Football Conference](/wiki/American_Football_Conference "American Football Conference") (AFC) when the NFL completed its merger with the AFL in [1970](/wiki/1970_NFL_season "1970 NFL season"):

* [Boston Patriots](/wiki/Boston_Patriots "Boston Patriots"), now the New England Patriots
* [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills")
* [Dallas Texans](/wiki/Dallas_Texans_(AFL) "Dallas Texans (AFL)"), now the Kansas City Chiefs
* [Denver Broncos](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos")
* [Houston Oilers](/wiki/Houston_Oilers "Houston Oilers"), now the Tennessee Titans
* [Los Angeles Chargers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Chargers "Los Angeles Chargers") for a year, then became the [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers"), but now are the Los Angeles Chargers again
* [Titans of New York](/wiki/Titans_of_New_York "Titans of New York"), now the New York Jets
* [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders") (Originally a [Minnesota team](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings"), then the botched-Spanglish Oakland Senors, before settling on Raiders prior to first game)

The Bills and Jets used AFL-era [throwback uniforms](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") as their alternate jerseys prior to 2009, and continued them beyond that. The league had the other six teams use a third jersey replicating those from the AFL in their heyday. Each of the Original Eight played against another original AFL team, one at home and one away, on two "AFL Legacy Weekends", and had a special 50th Anniversary AFL patch on the throwback uniforms. In a waiver given by these eight, they would wear the anniversary throwbacks a maximum of four times a season, whereas all of the other teams have a limit of twice per season. The Patriots and Raiders wore the throwbacks the maximum four times, while the Bills, Chiefs (Texans), Chargers, Jets (Titans), and Titans (Oilers) were worn three times. The Chargers also wore their regular powder blue [alternate jersey](/wiki/Third_jersey "Third jersey") of the current design for two other allowed games.[[48]](#cite_note-61) In the second game of two meetings opposite of the first game for their divisional matches against the Raiders and Chiefs, the Chargers wore their newer look powder blue jerseys against the Raiders in week 8, and also celebrated their 50th Anniversary in the modern powder blue jerseys against the Chiefs later in the season during Week 12, when the meeting against those two clubs shifted to San Diego. The Broncos elected to wear their throwbacks only twice (the team wore the infamous 1960–1961 brown and yellow throwbacks with the vertically-striped socks) and wore their regular orange alternate jersey of the current design in the other two allowed games,[[49]](#cite_note-62) giving the team six different uniforms over a 16-game season. Both of the games involving the orange jerseys involved the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") and the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers")—two "old" NFL teams before the merger, with the Steelers joining the Broncos in the AFC in 1970 as a result of the merger.

An AFL patch is already a permanent part of the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs")' jerseys, in honor of team and AFL founder [Lamar Hunt](/wiki/Lamar_Hunt "Lamar Hunt"), who died in December 2006. The program kicked off on August 9 in the 2009 [Pro Football Hall of Fame Game](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame_Game "Pro Football Hall of Fame Game") with the Bills playing the Titans (Oilers). Besides the Legacy Games, the Chiefs wore Dallas Texans uniforms in one home game against the NFC's Dallas Cowboys, who also wore throwbacks, while the Cowboys hosted the Raiders in a [Thanksgiving Day game](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day") in [Arlington, Texas](/wiki/Arlington,_Texas "Arlington, Texas"). (The Chiefs and Cowboys throwbacks both feature team-colored jerseys, making it the first dark color vs. dark color game since the Bears-Cowboys 2004 Thanksgiving game.) For all games other than those cited above, the Original Eight wore their 2009 uniforms, each with a team-specific 50th Anniversary shoulder patch, save for the Titans, who wore the AFL 50th Anniversary logo on all uniforms. For the legacy weekends, on-field officials working the Original Eight's games also had their own throwbacks – shirts with Chinese-red stripes, and an AFL chest and cap logo. The fields for the regular season games were painted in the innovative designs introduced by the AFL in the 1960s (for instance, the Broncos' end zones are painted in an [argyle](/wiki/Argyle_(pattern) "Argyle (pattern)") pattern).

Although the Dolphins and Bengals were both part of the AFL, they did not join the league until 1966 and 1968, respectively. The Dolphins played in three of the Legacy games (wearing their current uniforms), while the Bengals were not part of the events.

After the season, the Patriots adopted their AFL-era throwbacks as their new [third uniform](/wiki/Third_jersey "Third jersey") for 2010, while the Broncos continue to paint their end zones at [Invesco Field at Mile High](/wiki/Invesco_Field_at_Mile_High "Invesco Field at Mile High") in the argyle pattern, similar to the Steelers decision to paint the south end zone at [Heinz Field](/wiki/Heinz_Field "Heinz Field") in plain diagonal white lines after their 2003 preseason game against the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") honoring the 60th anniversary of the [Steagles](/wiki/Steagles "Steagles") season.

## Other anniversaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Other anniversaries")]

It was the fortieth season since the [AFL–NFL merger](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93NFL_merger "AFL–NFL merger") was officially completed in 1970, and also the sixtieth season since the [All-America Football Conference](/wiki/All-America_Football_Conference "All-America Football Conference") merged with the NFL, adding the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns") and [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") to the league.[[51]](#cite_note-64) No celebrations were held for any of those teams, but San Francisco reverted to their old colors (lighter shades of red and gold from their glory seasons of five Super Bowl victories) in an unrelated move.

### Dallas Cowboys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Dallas Cowboys")]

The NFC's [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") also celebrated their fiftieth season in 2009. After the NFL had rebuffed [Lamar Hunt](/wiki/Lamar_Hunt "Lamar Hunt")'s overtures to place an NFL team in Dallas, saying they had no plans to expand, the league granted the Cowboys a franchise in 1960 in reaction to Hunt's AFL Dallas Texans. The NFL's Cowboys franchise started out in [1960](/wiki/1960_NFL_season "1960 NFL season") with a record of no wins, eleven losses and one tie, but has since gone on to appear in eight [Super Bowls](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") (the only other teams to appear in eight was the [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") on February 6, 2011, the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") on February 1, 2015), and the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos") on February 7, 2016, winning five of them, tied for second with the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") and the New England Patriots, behind only the Pittsburgh Steelers. Ironically, the Texans' franchise, which left Dallas to become the Kansas City Chiefs, won [Super Bowl IV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_IV "Super Bowl IV"), two years before the Cowboys won [their first](/wiki/Super_Bowl_VI "Super Bowl VI"). Though there was never an actual game between the Dallas Texans and the Cowboys, the 2009 "throwback" game played against Kansas City (Dallas Texans) and the Dallas Cowboys was played as "The Game that Never Was". The [Dallas] Cowboys won the game at Arrowhead Stadium in Kansas City, Missouri, 26–20 in overtime. Interestingly, the visiting Cowboys wore home throwback jerseys, so both teams wore home uniforms.

### NFC North

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: NFC North")]

All four members of the [NFC North](/wiki/NFC_North "NFC North") celebrated significant anniversaries.

The [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") and [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears"), two of the oldest teams of the NFL remaining in the league, marked their 90th seasons in 2009. While the Packers did not join the NFL until 1921, the team marks its founding with the team's creation in 1919. The first *[Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football")* game of the season featured the two teams, pitted in "[The League's Oldest Rivalry](/wiki/Bears-Packers_rivalry "Bears-Packers rivalry")".

Likewise, the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings") celebrated their being in existence for 50 years. The team traces its existence to the founding of the AFL in 1959, but although they participated in that league's [inaugural draft](/wiki/1960_American_Football_League_Draft "1960 American Football League Draft"), they were instead lured to the NFL before playing a game. (The Oakland Raiders replaced Minnesota in the AFL.) The Minnesota team, not named the Vikings until after they joined the NFL and did not take any of their drafted players with them, were granted their own [expansion draft](/wiki/1961_NFL_Expansion_Draft "1961 NFL Expansion Draft") by the NFL and did not play until 1961. As such, the Vikings were only in their 49th season, and will celebrate their 50th NFL season in [2010](/wiki/2010_NFL_season "2010 NFL season"), while the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions") will be in its 80th season in the NFL, and their 76th in Detroit. Last season, Detroit reintroduced its throwback jersey, while Minnesota continues using theirs. None of the teams held significant celebrations in 2009.

## Notable events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Notable events")]

### Return of Brett Favre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Return of Brett Favre")]

After one season with the [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets"), [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre") retired again from football on February 11, 2009, and was released on April 28, 2009, making him a free agent. On May 4, 2009, rumors began nationwide on the Internet, radio and TV outlets about him coming out of retirement again and possibly joining the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings"), his division arch-rival when he was with the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers"), also meeting with Vikings head coach [Brad Childress](/wiki/Brad_Childress "Brad Childress") that week. On May 11, in an indication of Favre's possible return, it was reported he was scheduled to have a procedure on his torn biceps tendon on his throwing arm he injured when he was with the Jets and it was either a surgical or non-surgical process but wasn't confirmed throughout May. On June 15, 2009, he revealed he had surgery on his right torn biceps tendon and considered on playing again. On July 15, 2009, he informed the Vikings that he would make his decision of coming out of retirement or not by July 30, 2009, the day Vikings training camp started. On July 28, 2009, two days early before training camp, he informed the Vikings that he would remain retired. On August 18, 2009, it was reported that Favre got on a private jet and was heading to Minnesota to join the Vikings. It was later confirmed and he officially signed with the Minnesota Vikings. He was signed to a two-year, $25 million deal with an option for 2010 for $13 million.[[52]](#cite_note-65) On December 6, 2009, Favre played in his 283rd consecutive game, breaking [Jim Marshall](/wiki/Jim_Marshall_(defensive_end) "Jim Marshall (defensive end)")'s long-standing record. The Vikings finished their season with a record of 12–4 and made the playoffs as the number two seed. Favre was voted to the [2010 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2010_Pro_Bowl "2010 Pro Bowl"), but did not participate and was replaced by [Tony Romo](/wiki/Tony_Romo "Tony Romo").

### Return of Michael Vick

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Return of Michael Vick")]

Free agent quarterback [Michael Vick](/wiki/Michael_Vick "Michael Vick") was reinstated on July 27, 2009, after finishing his 2-year prison term and on August 13, 2009, he signed a one-year, $1.6 million contract with the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") also with an option in 2010 for $5 million.[[53]](#cite_note-66) Vick finished the season with one passing touchdown and two rushing touchdowns. He helped the Eagles to an 11–5 record and the NFC's number six seed.

### Jay Cutler trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Jay Cutler trade")]

After disagreements between [Jay Cutler](/wiki/Jay_Cutler "Jay Cutler") and the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos") management, on March 15, 2009, Cutler requested a trade from the team. On April 2, 2009, Cutler was traded to the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears") from the Broncos along with a fifth-round pick in the [2009 NFL draft](/wiki/2009_NFL_draft "2009 NFL draft"). In return, the Broncos acquired quarterback [Kyle Orton](/wiki/Kyle_Orton "Kyle Orton") along with the Bears' first and third-round selections in 2009 also the first round pick in 2010. Later in the season on October 20, 2009, the Bears signed Cutler to a two-year, $30 million contract extension up to 2013.

### Two teams undefeated late into the season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Two teams undefeated late into the season")]

The [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") started the season 14–0, and the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") started the season 13–0, the first time that two teams went that deep into the season without suffering a loss. Unlike the Saints who had not yet clinched home field throughout the playoffs and who legitimately lost their fourteenth game in Week 15, the Colts intentionally lost their fifteenth game in Week 16, giving an opportunity to the Jets to get into the playoffs. After setting the record for consecutive regular season wins over multiple seasons, running their mark to 23, the Colts were up at halftime at home, and then Coach Caldwell benched all his starters for the second half, allowing the Jets to win, and ruining the Colts' try at a perfect season. Indianapolis fans were incensed and heartily booed Caldwell.[[54]](#cite_note-67)

### Incidents with Tom Cable

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Incidents with Tom Cable")]

On August 17, 2009 [Oakland Raiders](/wiki/Oakland_Raiders "Oakland Raiders") head coach [Tom Cable](/wiki/Tom_Cable "Tom Cable") was accused of punching assistant coach [Randy Hanson](/wiki/Randy_Hanson "Randy Hanson") in the face and fracturing his jaw. The incident allegedly took place on August 5 during the Raiders training camp, held in Napa. On October 22, 2009, the Napa district attorney announced that no charges would be filed against Cable.[[55]](#cite_note-68) The Raiders finished their season with a record of 5–11.

### First Super Bowl appearance for Saints

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: First Super Bowl appearance for Saints")]

On January 24, 2010, the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") defeated the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings") in the NFC Championship Game in overtime by a score of 31–28. The win secured their berth in [Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV"), the franchise's first ever Super Bowl.

Later on February 7, the Saints defeated the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") in the Super Bowl by a score of 31–17. [Quarterback](/wiki/Quarterback "Quarterback") [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") was named the MVP.

## Records and milestones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Records and milestones")]

### Winning and losing streaks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Winning and losing streaks")]

* [The Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2009_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2009 Atlanta Falcons season") accomplished their first back to back winning seasons in franchise history. They still hold the record among all major American sports leagues for the longest streak of seasons without consecutive winning seasons. The streak lasted for 43 years from [1966](/wiki/1966_Atlanta_Falcons_season "1966 Atlanta Falcons season").
* [The New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2009_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2009 New Orleans Saints season") won their first 13 games in the NFC of the regular season, breaking the record of 12 straight by the [1985 Chicago Bears](/wiki/1985_Chicago_Bears "1985 Chicago Bears") who also won the Super Bowl.
* [The Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/2009_Indianapolis_Colts_season "2009 Indianapolis Colts season") won their 23rd consecutive regular season game with a 35–31 victory over [the Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/2009_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2009 Jacksonville Jaguars season") on December 16, 2009. The previous week, the Colts had broken the previous record (21-games) held by [the 2003–2004 New England Patriots](/wiki/2004_New_England_Patriots_season "2004 New England Patriots season"). They lost the following week to [the New York Jets](/wiki/2009_New_York_Jets_season "2009 New York Jets season") after resting many of their starters with a 15–10 lead in the third quarter of that game.[[57]](#cite_note-70)
* [The Denver Broncos](/wiki/2009_Denver_Broncos_season "2009 Denver Broncos season") became the third team since 1970 to miss the NFL playoffs after a 6–0 start. The other two teams to miss the playoffs since the AFL/NFL merger were [the 1978 Washington Redskins](/wiki/1978_Washington_Redskins_season "1978 Washington Redskins season") and [the 2003 Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/2003_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2003 Minnesota Vikings season").[[58]](#cite_note-71)
* [The Tennessee Titans](/wiki/2009_Tennessee_Titans_season "2009 Tennessee Titans season") started the season losing their first six games, then, led by second-string quarterback [Vince Young](/wiki/Vince_Young "Vince Young"), won their next five games, the first NFL team to have such a turnaround.[[59]](#cite_note-72)
* [The Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/2009_Pittsburgh_Steelers_season "2009 Pittsburgh Steelers season") became the first team since [the 1987 New York Giants](/wiki/1987_New_York_Giants_season "1987 New York Giants season") to lose five consecutive games in the season after winning [the Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIII "Super Bowl XLIII").
* Two teams tied an NFL record for most kickoff returns for touchdowns in a single game, with two: [the Miami Dolphins](/wiki/2009_Miami_Dolphins_season "2009 Miami Dolphins season") (November 1, vs. [the New York Jets](/wiki/2009_New_York_Jets_season "2009 New York Jets season")) and [the Cleveland Browns](/wiki/2009_Cleveland_Browns_season "2009 Cleveland Browns season") (December 20, vs. [the Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/2009_Kansas_City_Chiefs_season "2009 Kansas City Chiefs season")).

### Rams achieve worst peacetime three-season streak until mid-2010s Browns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Rams achieve worst peacetime three-season streak until mid-2010s Browns")]

[The St. Louis Rams](/wiki/History_of_the_St._Louis_Rams "History of the St. Louis Rams") finish as the NFL's tenth 1–15 or 0–16 team, giving them a record from [2007](/wiki/2007_NFL_season "2007 NFL season") to 2009 of six wins and forty-two losses. Since a regular schedule began [in 1936](/wiki/1936_NFL_season "1936 NFL season"), only two teams have achieved a comparably bad record over three seasons:

1. [the wartime Cardinals](/wiki/List_of_Arizona_Cardinals_seasons "List of Arizona Cardinals seasons") going 1–29 between [1943](/wiki/1943_NFL_season "1943 NFL season") and [1945](/wiki/1945_NFL_season "1945 NFL season") if one includes [Card-Pitt](/wiki/Card-Pitt "Card-Pitt"), and 4–27 if one only includes Cardinals seasons.
2. [the 2015](/wiki/2015_Cleveland_Browns_season "2015 Cleveland Browns season") to [2017 Cleveland Browns](/wiki/2017_Cleveland_Browns_season "2017 Cleveland Browns season"), who went 4–44 over three seasons

### First team to go to the Super Bowl with a losing streak

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: First team to go to the Super Bowl with a losing streak")]

[The New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2009_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2009 New Orleans Saints season") became the first team in NFL history to lose their last three regular season games and then go on to the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") and win.

### Two top seeds face each other in Super Bowl

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Two top seeds face each other in Super Bowl")]

For the first time since [the 1993 season](/wiki/1993_NFL_season "1993 NFL season"), the AFC's and NFC's top seeds, the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts") and the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") respectively, played one another in the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV"), where the Saints defeated the Colts, 31–17.

### Tom Brady's record-setting quarter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Tom Brady's record-setting quarter")]

In a Week 6 game against [the Tennessee Titans](/wiki/2009_Tennessee_Titans_season "2009 Tennessee Titans season"), the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots")' [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") threw five touchdown passes in the second quarter, an NFL record. The Patriots led the Titans 45–0 at halftime, also a league record, before winning the game 59–0, tied for the league's largest shutout margin since the 1970 [AFL–NFL merger](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93NFL_merger "AFL–NFL merger").[[60]](#cite_note-73)

### Panthers produce two 1,100+ yard rushers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Panthers produce two 1,100+ yard rushers")]

Further information: [List of NFL teams with multiple 1000 yard rushers](/wiki/List_of_NFL_teams_with_multiple_1000_yard_rushers "List of NFL teams with multiple 1000 yard rushers")

[DeAngelo Williams](/wiki/DeAngelo_Williams "DeAngelo Williams") and [Jonathan Stewart](/wiki/Jonathan_Stewart "Jonathan Stewart") of the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers") became the first teammates in NFL history to rush for 1,100 yards in the same season. Williams rushed for 1,117 yards, and Stewart ran for 1,133 yards.

### Kurt Warner sets single-game regular-season completion percentage record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Kurt Warner sets single-game regular-season completion percentage record")]

In Week 2, the [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals")' [Kurt Warner](/wiki/Kurt_Warner "Kurt Warner") set a new NFL record for completion percentage, completing 92.3% of his passes (24 completions in 26 attempts) in a 31–17 win over the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars"). The previous record had been set by [Vinny Testaverde](/wiki/Vinny_Testaverde "Vinny Testaverde") in [1993](/wiki/1993_NFL_season "1993 NFL season"). If postseason games are included, the record holder is [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") of the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots"), who completed 26 of 28 attempts on January 12, 2008, also against the Jaguars.

### Brandon Marshall breaks single-game reception record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Brandon Marshall breaks single-game reception record")]

In Week 13, the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos")' [Brandon Marshall](/wiki/Brandon_Marshall "Brandon Marshall") caught a record 21 catches in a losing effort against the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts"). (The record was previously held by the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers")' [Terrell Owens](/wiki/Terrell_Owens "Terrell Owens"), who had 20 catches in a [2001](/wiki/2001_NFL_season "2001 NFL season") game.)

### Aaron Rodgers strong start

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Aaron Rodgers strong start")]

[Aaron Rodgers](/wiki/Aaron_Rodgers "Aaron Rodgers") of the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") became the first quarterback to throw for 4,000 yards in each of his first two seasons as a starter. (Rodgers, Cam Newton of the Carolina Panthers, [Andrew Luck](/wiki/Andrew_Luck "Andrew Luck") of the [Indianapolis Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts"), and [Kurt Warner](/wiki/Kurt_Warner "Kurt Warner") of the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") are the only quarterbacks to throw for 4,000 in their first season as a starter. Warner, however, passed for only 3,429 in [2000](/wiki/2000_NFL_season "2000 NFL season"). Rodgers passed for 4,038 in [2008](/wiki/2008_NFL_season "2008 NFL season") and 4,434 in 2009.)[[61]](#cite_note-74)

### 2,000-yard season for Chris Johnson

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: 2,000-yard season for Chris Johnson")]

On January 3, 2010, [Chris Johnson](/wiki/Chris_Johnson_(running_back) "Chris Johnson (running back)") of the [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/Tennessee_Titans "Tennessee Titans") became the sixth rusher in NFL history to eclipse 2,000 rushing yards in a season. Johnson also broke the all-purpose yards from scrimmage record previously held by [Marshall Faulk](/wiki/Marshall_Faulk "Marshall Faulk").

### Joshua Cribbs breaks NFL's career returns record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Joshua Cribbs breaks NFL's career returns record")]

In a Week 15 game against the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") on December 20, 2009, the [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns")' [Joshua Cribbs](/wiki/Joshua_Cribbs "Joshua Cribbs") returned two kickoffs for touchdowns, giving him eight for his career, and setting a new league record. Cribbs also became one of only two players to score two 100-plus yard touchdowns in the same game. (The feat was first accomplished by [Ted Ginn Jr.](/wiki/Ted_Ginn_Jr. "Ted Ginn Jr.") of the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") in Week 8 of the 2009 season.)

### Cowboys set single-game attendance record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Cowboys set single-game attendance record")]

For the opening game of their new stadium, the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys") distributed 105,121 tickets, setting an NFL record for attendance in a single game. The old mark of 103,467 occurred in October 2005 at a 49ers-Cardinals game at [Azteca Stadium](/wiki/Azteca_Stadium "Azteca Stadium") in Mexico.

### Drew Brees sets season completion percentage record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Drew Brees sets season completion percentage record")]

[Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees") of the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") set an NFL record for [completion percentage in a season](/wiki/List_of_National_Football_League_annual_pass_completion_percentage_leaders "List of National Football League annual pass completion percentage leaders"). Brees was 363 of 514, a completion percentage of 70.6. (The record of 70.55% had previously been set in the strike-shortened [1982 season](/wiki/1982_NFL_season "1982 NFL season") by [Ken Anderson](/wiki/Ken_Anderson_(quarterback) "Ken Anderson (quarterback)") of the [Cincinnati Bengals](/wiki/Cincinnati_Bengals "Cincinnati Bengals"). Brees sat out the last game of the 2009 season since New Orleans had secured home-field advantage throughout the NFC Playoffs.)

### Highest scoring playoff game in NFL history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Highest scoring playoff game in NFL history")]

On [January 10, 2010](/wiki/2009_NFC_Wild_Card_playoff_game_(Green_Bay%E2%80%93Arizona) "2009 NFC Wild Card playoff game (Green Bay–Arizona)"), the [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals") defeated the [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers") 51–45 for a combined total of 96 points, setting a new NFL playoff record for total combined points scored.[[62]](#cite_note-75) This game has been given the nicknames "The Shootout", and the "Nobody Stopping Nobody Game".[[63]](#cite_note-76)

### Conference championships become most viewed playoff games in history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Conference championships become most viewed playoff games in history")]

On January 24, 2010, the NFC and AFC championship games averaged 52.9 million viewers, making it the most-viewed conference championship day since the two games in 1982 averaged 60.2 million viewers. [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company")'s telecast of the [Saints'](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") 31–28 overtime win over the [Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings") earned a 30.6 fast-national Nielsen rating (57.9 million viewers), marking Fox's biggest audience ever for an NFC championship game. It was also the second largest all-time audience for any conference title telecast, trailing only the [1982 49ers-Cowboys game](/wiki/1982_NFL_season "1982 NFL season") (68.7 million viewers on [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")).[[64]](#cite_note-sportingnews.com-77)

Excluding Super Bowl telecasts, the Saints-Vikings game was the most-viewed television program since the "[Seinfeld](/wiki/Seinfeld "Seinfeld")" finale in 1998. Meanwhile, CBS earned a 26.3 fast-national rating (46.9 million viewers) for the [Colts](/wiki/Indianapolis_Colts "Indianapolis Colts")–[Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets") AFC championship game in the early window, marking the largest audience for an AFC title game since [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") earned 47.5 million viewers for [Patriots–Dolphins](/wiki/1986_NFL_season "1986 NFL season") in 1986.[[64]](#cite_note-sportingnews.com-77)

### Super Bowl becomes most viewed program in history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Super Bowl becomes most viewed program in history")]

[Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV") surpassed the 1983 finale of [M\*A\*S\*H](/wiki/M*A*S*H_(TV_series) "M*A*S*H (TV series)"), as the most viewed program in history. It was watched by 153.4 million people.[[65]](#cite_note-78) Compelling story lines included the city of [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") and its ongoing recovery from [Hurricane Katrina](/wiki/Hurricane_Katrina "Hurricane Katrina"), as well as Colts quarterback [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning")'s attempt at a second [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") ring.

## Stadium changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Stadium changes")]

The 2009 season was the first season for the new [Cowboys Stadium](/wiki/Cowboys_Stadium "Cowboys Stadium") in [Arlington](/wiki/Arlington,_Texas "Arlington, Texas"), replacing [Texas Stadium](/wiki/Texas_Stadium "Texas Stadium") in [Irving](/wiki/Irving,_Texas "Irving, Texas").

Dolphin Stadium was renamed [Land Shark Stadium](/wiki/Land_Shark_Stadium "Land Shark Stadium") after a naming rights deal was signed with the Land Shark beer that was sold at [Jimmy Buffett](/wiki/Jimmy_Buffett "Jimmy Buffett")'s Margaritaville restaurant chain.

This season also served as the last season for [Giants Stadium](/wiki/Giants_Stadium "Giants Stadium"), as both the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") and [Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets") moved into [a new stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium") for 2010. The Giants exited with an auspicious 41–9 loss to Carolina on December 27, while the Jets defeated the Cincinnati Bengals by a 37–0 score in the season finale on Sunday night, January 3, 2010.

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

After having no major uniform changes for the [2008 NFL season](/wiki/2008_NFL_season "2008 NFL season"), the trend of at least one major uniform change per season among the 32 teams returned with two major uniform changes, as well as one with some minor modifications.

The [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers"), who had been long-rumored to be returning to their [1964](/wiki/1964_San_Francisco_49ers_season "1964 San Francisco 49ers season")–[1995](/wiki/1995_San_Francisco_49ers_season "1995 San Francisco 49ers season") uniforms (and have had the red variation of those uniforms as their [third uniform](/wiki/Third_jersey "Third jersey") since [the 2002 season](/wiki/2002_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2002 San Francisco 49ers season")) did so for [this season](/wiki/2009_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2009 San Francisco 49ers season").[[72]](#cite_note-85) The team returned to a brighter, scarlet red and a less metallic "49ers gold" as its team colors, replacing the darker cardinal red and the more metallic "49ers gold" which the team has worn since it last overhauled their uniforms in [1996](/wiki/1996_San_Francisco_49ers_season "1996 San Francisco 49ers season").[[73]](#cite_note-86) The new uniforms were unveiled on April 25, 2009.

The [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars") got new uniforms for [the 2009 season](/wiki/2009_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2009 Jacksonville Jaguars season").[[74]](#cite_note-JaxUni-87)[[75]](#cite_note-AskVic-88) Team owner [Wayne Weaver](/wiki/Wayne_Weaver "Wayne Weaver") reportedly wanted to "clean up" the look, feeling that the team has too many uniform styles.[[75]](#cite_note-AskVic-88) The changes aren't a complete overhaul, but similar to the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/Atlanta_Falcons "Atlanta Falcons") and [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings")' recent overhauls.[[74]](#cite_note-JaxUni-87) The new uniforms were introduced at a press conference on April 22.[[76]](#cite_note-89)

The [Detroit Lions](/wiki/Detroit_Lions "Detroit Lions") designated their popular 1950s-era throwbacks as their [third uniform](/wiki/Third_jersey "Third jersey") and completely dropped their unpopular black jerseys.[[77]](#cite_note-noblack-90) The team had not worn the throwbacks from 2005 to 2007 to make room for the [Matt Millen](/wiki/Matt_Millen "Matt Millen")-designated black jerseys. In addition, the team unveiled a new helmet logo on April 20, updating "Bubbles" with a fiercer look and wordmark, with modified uniforms. Those logos were accidentally leaked by NFL.com in their online shop, then quickly removed on March 23, 2009.[[78]](#cite_note-91)

Besides the above-mentioned [throwbacks](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") for the 50th anniversary season of the AFL, the [Tampa Bay Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") wore their "[Creamsicle](/wiki/Creamsicle "Creamsicle")" throwbacks for the November 8 home game against the Green Bay Packers, in conjunction with the creation of the Buccaneers Ring of Honor.[[79]](#cite_note-92) The Buccaneers defeated the Packers 38–28, the only home game the team won in 2009.

The [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams") wore their [1973](/wiki/1973_Los_Angeles_Rams_season "1973 Los Angeles Rams season")–[1999](/wiki/1999_St._Louis_Rams_season "1999 St. Louis Rams season") blue uniforms in select home games to honor former owner [Georgia Frontiere](/wiki/Georgia_Frontiere "Georgia Frontiere"), who died in early 2008 (too late to inform the NFL about wearing a throwback uniform in her honor during [the 2008 season](/wiki/2008_St._Louis_Rams_season "2008 St. Louis Rams season"); the team instead opted for a memorial patch) and also celebrate the tenth anniversary of their win in [Super Bowl XXXIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXIV "Super Bowl XXXIV").[[80]](#cite_note-93) Ironically, the Rams [cut ties](/wiki/Fire_sale_(sports) "Fire sale (sports)") with its last two remaining offensive players from the "[Greatest Show on Turf](/wiki/The_Greatest_Show_on_Turf "The Greatest Show on Turf")" era during the offseason by releasing [Orlando Pace](/wiki/Orlando_Pace "Orlando Pace") and [Torry Holt](/wiki/Torry_Holt "Torry Holt") for [salary cap](/wiki/Salary_cap "Salary cap") reasons plus opting for a rebuilding mode after a 2–14 season in 2008 (second only to [the Lions](/wiki/2008_Detroit_Lions_season "2008 Detroit Lions season")' [historic 0–16 season](/wiki/Imperfect_season "Imperfect season")) and no trips to the postseason since [the 2004 season](/wiki/2004_St._Louis_Rams_season "2004 St. Louis Rams season"). [Defensive end](/wiki/Defensive_end "Defensive end") [Leonard Little](/wiki/Leonard_Little "Leonard Little") is the only player remaining who wore the pre-2000 uniforms again in 2009. These uniforms were worn on October 11 against the Vikings and December 20 against the Texans.

The [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns"), [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/Dallas_Cowboys "Dallas Cowboys"), [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings"), and [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") retained their throwback alternates worn in previous seasons. The [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/Atlanta_Falcons "Atlanta Falcons") wore replicas of their first season uniforms from [1966](/wiki/1966_NFL_season "1966 NFL season"), in lieu of their all-black uniforms. The Tennessee Titans wore a Number 9 [decal](/wiki/Decal "Decal") on the back of their helmets to honor former Titans quarterback [Steve McNair](/wiki/Steve_McNair "Steve McNair"), who was killed on July 4, while the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") wore a decal with the initials of [Jim Johnson](/wiki/Jim_Johnson_(American_football) "Jim Johnson (American football)"), their longtime defensive coordinator who died on July 28. The [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks") also unveiled a [lime green](/wiki/Lime_green "Lime green") jersey with blue shoulders, to pay tribute to [the new Major League Soccer team](/wiki/Seattle_Sounders_FC "Seattle Sounders FC") with whom they share [Qwest Field](/wiki/Qwest_Field "Qwest Field"). The jersey No. 71 was retired for former offensive lineman [Walter Jones](/wiki/Walter_Jones_(American_football) "Walter Jones (American football)") on December 9, 2009.

The [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins"), after a four-year hiatus, brought back their alternate orange jerseys and wore them against the New York Jets on October 12.

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Media")]

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Television")]

Further information: [NFL on television](/wiki/NFL_on_television "NFL on television")

This was the fourth season under the [television contracts](/wiki/NFL_on_television "NFL on television") with the league's television partners: [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS") (all AFC Sunday afternoon away games and one [Thanksgiving game](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day")), [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox") (all NFC Sunday afternoon away games and one Thanksgiving game), [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football") (16 Sunday night games and the kickoff game), [ESPN](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football") (17 Monday night games over sixteen weeks), [NFL Network](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football") (eight late-season games on Thursday and Saturday nights, including one Thanksgiving game and a [Christmas](/wiki/National_Football_League_Christmas_games "National Football League Christmas games") night game), and [DirecTV](/wiki/DirecTV "DirecTV")'s [NFL Sunday Ticket](/wiki/NFL_Sunday_Ticket "NFL Sunday Ticket") package. These agreements with CBS, Fox and DirecTV were extended two years through the 2013 season on May 19, 2009, and NBC's contract was also extended through that same season on August 19 of the same year; these extensions were done so they all coincided with the 2013 expiration date on ESPN's contract.

[CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS") celebrated their 50th season of NFL coverage; CBS has covered NFL games from 1956 to 1993 and again from 1998 to the present. Ironically, CBS which was ordered by the NFL not to give American Football League scores during its NFL broadcasts of the 1960s, now covers the AFC, while Fox covers the NFC. This season was also the fortieth consecutive season that *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")* has been a permanent part of the NFL schedule, though the league had played games on Monday night sporadically before this. *Monday Night Football* originally aired on [ABC](/wiki/ESPN_on_ABC "ESPN on ABC") before switching to [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") in 2006, when the two networks' sports operations were merged. The first Monday night of the regular season featured two AFL Original Eight games, a doubleheader with the Bills at the Patriots and the Chargers at the Raiders.

[NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network") continued to have coverage disputes with major cable providers. In particular, [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast"), the largest cable provider in the United States, was considering removing the network from its lineups on April 30, 2009, shortly after the draft but before the start of the preseason. Comcast was carrying the network on a digital sports tier and negotiations continued past the April 30 deadline as NFLN would continue on Comcast, which ended with a resolution on May 19 that could open the door for other major cable providers such as [Cablevision](/wiki/Cablevision "Cablevision") and [Cox](/wiki/Cox_Communications "Cox Communications") to carry the network on a what would be equal to Comcast's digital classic tier, with around 10 million subscribers.[[81]](#cite_note-94) However, the most notable holdout, [Time Warner Cable](/wiki/Time_Warner_Cable "Time Warner Cable"), still is nowhere near a deal.[[82]](#cite_note-95) Additionally, the NFL Network created a new "Red Zone Channel" starting with the season openers September 13. Comcast-owned [Versus](/wiki/Versus_(TV_channel) "Versus (TV channel)") has signed a deal to carry [United Football League](/wiki/United_Football_League_(2009) "United Football League (2009)") games on Thursday nights; the tail end of the UFL schedule overlapped with the first few weeks of the Thursday night NFL package. In related news, the NFL has reached a settlement with [DISH Network](/wiki/DISH_Network "DISH Network") over the satellite provider's decision to move NFL Network to a higher tier. NFL Network has also dropped the use of the names "Run to the Playoffs" and "Saturday Night Football", opting to standardize all of its broadcasts under the "Thursday Night Football" banner. The Saturday night and Friday night games airing on the network was marketed as "Thursday Night Football Special Edition".

This was also the first NFL season after the [DTV transition in the United States](/wiki/DTV_transition_in_the_United_States "DTV transition in the United States"), which had originally been scheduled to take place on February 17, 2009, but was delayed until June 12, 2009. Hawaiʻi [made the digital switchover](/wiki/List_of_television_stations_in_Hawaii#Early_conversion_to_DT "List of television stations in Hawaii") on January 15, 2009.[[83]](#cite_note-96) (Low-power translators will still be allowed to broadcast in analog until at least 2012, and cable providers will continue to distribute analog signals for the foreseeable future.)

After fifty seasons as a player, coach, broadcaster and video game maven, [John Madden](/wiki/John_Madden "John Madden") retired on April 16 from his position on *Sunday Night Football*. [Cris Collinsworth](/wiki/Cris_Collinsworth "Cris Collinsworth") moved from NFL Network to NBC to assume Madden's in-game analyst role with [Al Michaels](/wiki/Al_Michaels "Al Michaels"); [Matt Millen](/wiki/Matt_Millen "Matt Millen") replaced Collinsworth on NFL Network, while [Tony Dungy](/wiki/Tony_Dungy "Tony Dungy") and [Rodney Harrison](/wiki/Rodney_Harrison "Rodney Harrison") replaced Collinsworth and [Jerome Bettis](/wiki/Jerome_Bettis "Jerome Bettis") as studio analysts on NBC's *[Football Night in America](/wiki/Football_Night_in_America "Football Night in America")* pregame show. In addition, [Tony Kornheiser](/wiki/Tony_Kornheiser "Tony Kornheiser") left *MNF* and former Raiders and [Buccaneers](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers "Tampa Bay Buccaneers") coach [Jon Gruden](/wiki/Jon_Gruden "Jon Gruden") replaced him.

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_NFL_season&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Radio")]

In radio, it was reported that the league was exploring ending its contract with [Westwood One](/wiki/NFL_on_Westwood_One "NFL on Westwood One") or sharing games with another network due to Westwood One's financial problems. The [Sports USA Radio Network](/wiki/Sports_USA_Radio_Network "Sports USA Radio Network"), [ESPN Radio](/wiki/ESPN_Radio "ESPN Radio") and [Sporting News Radio](/wiki/Sporting_News_Radio "Sporting News Radio") were mentioned as possible partners; [Fox Sports Radio](/wiki/Fox_Sports_Radio "Fox Sports Radio") was notably excluded from consideration. Sports USA currently carries Sunday afternoon games by agreement with individual teams, while ESPN carries the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") and [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball"), the latter causing a potential schedule conflict between [Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football") and [Sunday Night Baseball](/wiki/Sunday_Night_Baseball "Sunday Night Baseball"), plus the MLB Playoffs. Sunday night, [Monday night](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football"), [Thanksgiving](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day") and all other [Thursday and Saturday](/wiki/Run_to_the_Playoffs "Run to the Playoffs") games are covered by the contract.[[84]](#cite_note-97) Of the offers, Westwood One was the high bidder (and reportedly the only one offering guaranteed money), ESPN requested a longer-term deal, the Sporting News offered a revenue-sharing plan in lieu of rights fees, and Sports USA was described as a "long shot".[[85]](#cite_note-98) After a restructuring shored up the company's financial situation, Westwood One in March 2009 earned a two-year extension for all of the night games, paying US$33,000,000 for the two-year deal.[[86]](#cite_note-99)

In addition to the official feature game package, three networks will carry nationwide radio broadcasts of Sunday afternoon games. The newest such network is [Compass Media Networks](/wiki/Compass_Media_Networks "Compass Media Networks"), which has signed deals with eight teams. Sports USA and Westwood One will carry games from the other 24 teams. [Dial Global](/wiki/Dial_Global "Dial Global"), which previously backed Sports USA's coverage in 2008, will instead handle Compass's package for 2009.
